description:
You are acting as a junior backup-integrity engineer.  
A recent nightly script copied the production data directory 
  /home/user/project_data  
to the backup location  
  /home/user/backup/project_data_copy  

Management now needs a very small verification report that proves the backup is byte-for-byte identical to the source.

Your goal  
1. Compare SHA-256 checksums of every regular file that exists in both directories.  (There are exactly two files: data1.txt and data2.txt.)  
2. Create a plain-text log file named  
   /home/user/backup_verification.log  
   that follows the **exact format** shown below.

Required log format (including capitalisation, colons, spaces and new-lines) – the automated grader will compare this file byte-for-byte:

FILE VERIFICATION REPORT  
data1.txt: <ORIGINAL_SHA256> == <BACKUP_SHA256> : <STATUS>  
data2.txt: <ORIGINAL_SHA256> == <BACKUP_SHA256> : <STATUS>  
OVERALL STATUS: <FINAL_RESULT>

Where  
• <STATUS> is either OK (hashes match) or MISMATCH (hashes differ).  
• <FINAL_RESULT> is PASSED if every line’s status is OK, otherwise FAILED.  
• Keep the files listed in **alphabetical order** exactly as above.  
• End the file with a single trailing newline.

Success criteria  
• /home/user/backup_verification.log exists.  
• Its content matches the specification exactly.  
• No extra lines, spaces or characters are present.  
• The overall status must be PASSED when all hashes match; FAILED otherwise.

You only need 2–3 sensible shell commands to achieve this.  
Do not rename, delete, or move any existing files; just verify and log.
truth:
Pre-existing state before the agent begins:

1. Directory structure
   /home/user/
     ├── project_data/
     │     ├── data1.txt
     │     └── data2.txt
     └── backup/
           └── project_data_copy/
                 ├── data1.txt
                 └── data2.txt

2. File contents  
   /home/user/project_data/data1.txt            →  abc  
   /home/user/project_data/data2.txt            →  test  
   /home/user/backup/project_data_copy/data1.txt (identical to source)  
   /home/user/backup/project_data_copy/data2.txt (identical to source)  

3. Reference SHA-256 values used by the grader  
   data1.txt → ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad  
   data2.txt → 9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08  

4. Expected correct report content (including final newline):

FILE VERIFICATION REPORT
data1.txt: ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad == ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad : OK
data2.txt: 9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08 == 9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08 : OK
OVERALL STATUS: PASSED

The grader will:
• Re-calculate hashes for each file in both directories.  
• Ensure they match the hard-coded values above.  
• Confirm /home/user/backup_verification.log exists and its content is **exactly** as shown.

The agent must NOT see the above values directly; it must derive them via normal shell utilities.
name: task_000000_b61ff946
